metadata.summary: WCAG 2.2 compliance baseline by 2025/2026: focus visibility, target size 24x24px, drag alternatives, accessible authentication, and six new success criteria.

content.title: WCAG 2.2 Compliance
content.summary: WCAG 2.2 released October 2023, baseline compliance standard by 2025-2026. Covers nine new success criteria including focus visibility, target size, drag alternatives, and accessible authentication.

why.p: WCAG 2.2 introduces nine new success criteria targeting modern interaction patterns (dragging, authentication, help consistency) and real-world failures that earlier versions missed. It is the global baseline compliance standard by 2025-2026 for government and enterprise procurements. Automated scanners catch only 30-40% of issues; manual validation and assistive-technology testing remain essential.

list.item: Validating a web product against WCAG 2.2 AA — the global baseline by 2025/2026.
Adding a CI gate that flags new violations of the nine WCAG 2.2 success criteria added on top of 2.1.
Auditing focus visibility, target size, dragging, accessible authentication, redundant entry, consistent help.
Preparing VPAT 2.5 / ACR conformance documentation.
Native mobile-only apps without web surfaces — defer to platform a11y APIs (UIAccessibility, Android a11y).
Internal tooling with a documented narrow user pool that does not include disabled users (rare; document the decision).
WCAG 3.0 silver-bullet conversations — 3.0 is still working draft; do not retire 2.2 baselines.
Pure visual design review — heuristics and cognitive walkthrough cover that better.

section.name: What's New in WCAG 2.2
section.p: Nine new success criteria released October 2023 as part of the WCAG 2.2 recommendation.

rule.statement: All nine new WCAG 2.2 success criteria are considered baseline accessibility requirements and must be tested before launch.
rule.rationale: Each new SC targets a real-world failure mode that earlier versions missed. Together they address focus indicators, target size, modern interaction patterns, and authentication barriers.

example.code:
WCAG 2.2 New Success Criteria:
2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured (Min) — AA Focus indicator must be at least partially visible.
2.4.12 Focus Not Obscured (Enhanced) — AAA Focus indicator fully visible.
2.4.13 Focus Appearance — AAA 2px minimum focus indicator.
2.5.7 Dragging Movements — AA All drag actions have non-drag alternative.
2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum) — AA 24x24 CSS pixels minimum.
3.2.6 Consistent Help — A Help mechanism in same location across pages.
3.3.7 Redundant Entry — A Don't ask for same info twice.
3.3.8 Accessible Authentication (Min) — AA No cognitive function tests for login.
3.3.9 Accessible Authentication (Enhanced) — AAA No object/image recognition for login.

section.name: Target Size Requirements

rule.statement: All interactive targets must be at least 24x24 CSS pixels minimum, or 44x44 CSS pixels for touch targets (recommended).
rule.rationale: Users with motor control difficulties require larger targets; 24x24 is the minimum; 44x44 is best practice for mobile and touch interfaces.

section.note: Target size is computed including spacing exception; agents must read the SC carefully to avoid over-reporting.

section.name: Dragging Alternatives

rule.statement: Every drag action must have a non-drag alternative control. Ship the non-drag alternative first; add drag as enhancement only after keyboard/pointer alternatives exist.
rule.rationale: Drag-only interfaces exclude users with motor disabilities, tremors, and alternative input devices. Non-drag alternatives must be discoverable, not hidden.

example.code: Drag Action | Alternative Required ------------|-------------------- Drag to reorder list | Up/down buttons Drag to resize panel | Input field for value Drag to select area | Click corners + shift-click Drag to zoom map | + / - buttons

section.note: Drag alternatives often added as hidden controls that fail "discoverability" — passes scanner, fails users.

section.name: Focus Visibility

rule.statement: Document focus styles in design tokens (`--focus-ring-width`, `--focus-ring-color`) so 2.4.11/12/13 stay consistent across the site. Never use `outline: none` without a custom focus indicator.
rule.rationale: Default browser focus indicators vary; custom focus styles must be consistently visible, have sufficient contrast, and not be obscured by sticky headers or other UI.

section.note: Focus visibility depends on z-index and sticky headers at runtime — needs viewport + keyboard simulation testing. Automated scanners miss z-index violations.

section.name: Accessible Authentication

rule.statement: Authentication mechanisms must not require cognitive function tests (math, memory, recitation) or object/image recognition. Test authentication with no copy/paste, no autofill — captcha patterns now fail 3.3.8/9.
rule.rationale: Users with cognitive disabilities cannot reliably complete CAPTCHA or memory-based authentication. Allow login with username/password, biometric, or passkeys; never gatekeep with cognitive tests.

section.note: WCAG 3.0 guidance on authentication is still evolving; 2.2 baseline is the current standard.

rule.statement: Keep the "Help" link or contact mechanism in identical position site-wide (usually footer or persistent header). Cross-page reasoning is required to validate this — single-page scanners miss it.
rule.rationale: Users need to locate help quickly; moving it between pages adds cognitive load and breaks discoverability expectations.

list.item: Automated scanners catch ~30-40% of WCAG issues; manual + assistive-tech testing still required.
"Accessible Authentication (Min)" and "Consistent Help" need cross-page reasoning — single-page scanners miss them.
"Focus Not Obscured" depends on z-index / sticky headers at runtime — needs viewport + keyboard simulation.
Drag alternatives often added as hidden controls that fail "discoverability" — passes scanner, fails users.
Target size 24x24 CSS px is computed including spacing exception — agents misread the SC.
Color-contrast checks fail on gradients and images-of-text — need pixel sampling, not CSS reads.

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: Use a subagent to run axe-core / Pa11y / Lighthouse on each route, parse violations, and triage them against WCAG 2.2 SC IDs. A second agent walks new SCs (2.4.11, 2.4.12, 2.4.13, 2.5.7, 2.5.8, 3.2.6, 3.3.7, 3.3.8, 3.3.9) by writing Playwright keyboard scripts that inspect focus rect, target size, drag alternatives. A human reviews authentication and help-consistency findings.

list.item: `faion-usability-agent` — manual SC evaluation, screen-reader walk-throughs.
`faion-sdd-executor-agent` — patches code, adds CI a11y gates, runs axe in Playwright.
`faion-ux-researcher-agent` — recruits AT users for validation.
`axe-core` / `@axe-core/cli` — rule engine, ~57% of WCAG covered. Install: `npm i -g @axe-core/cli`; deque.com/axe
`pa11y` / `pa11y-ci` — headless WCAG scan, CI-friendly. Install: `npm i -g pa11y-ci`; pa11y.org
Lighthouse CI — full audit incl. a11y subset. Install: `npm i -g @lhci/cli`
`nu html-validator` — catches HTML errors that break a11y trees. validator.github.io/validator
`wcag-em-report-tool` — W3C evaluation report builder. github.com/w3c/wcag-em-report-tool
`playwright` + `@axe-core/playwright` — stateful, route-by-route a11y in CI. playwright.dev
Deque axe DevTools — SaaS+OSS, REST API, Pro tier adds Intelligent Guided Tests for 2.2 SCs.
Siteimprove — SaaS, REST API for crawl results, enterprise-grade tracking.
Tenon.io — SaaS, REST API, strong color-contrast logic.
Stark — SaaS plugin, Figma plugin first, API for some plans, good for design-time checks.
TPGi ARC — SaaS, REST API, manual + automated hybrid.
Avoid: Equally AI / accessiBe overlays — they often regress UX, not a substitute for fixes.
Run automated scans on every PR; fail on any new 2.2 SC violation, warn on existing.
Pair each automated check with one human AT walkthrough per release on top user flows.
Document focus styles in tokens (`--focus-ring-width`, `--focus-ring-color`) so 2.4.11/12/13 stay consistent.
For drag interactions, ship the non-drag alternative first; add drag as enhancement.
Keep "Help" link in identical position site-wide (3.2.6) — usually footer or persistent header.
Test authentication with no copy/paste, no autofill — many "captcha" patterns now fail 3.3.8/9.

antipattern.description: LLM assumes "accessible" means "alt text added"; 2.2 is mostly about interaction quality.
antipattern.reason: Accessibility spans semantic structure, keyboard navigation, focus management, cognitive load, color contrast, and more. Alt text is one small piece.

antipattern.description: Agent misses focus-indicator regressions caused by `outline: none` in design tokens.
antipattern.reason: Linting for `outline: none` without a paired custom focus indicator is essential; agents often remove outlines and forget to add custom styles.

antipattern.description: `aria-hidden` and `inert` get over-applied; agent must verify keyboard reachability after applying.
antipattern.reason: These attributes hide content from assistive tech; over-applying them removes keyboard access to interactive elements.

antipattern.description: Color-contrast checks fail on gradients and images-of-text — agent reads CSS only.
antipattern.reason: Pixel-level sampling is needed for gradient contrast; CSS reads cannot infer rendered colors. Images-of-text need OCR + contrast analysis.

antipattern.description: Target-size SC: agent does not include the spacing exception; over-reports icon buttons.
antipattern.reason: The SC allows spacing around the target; if 24x24 includes padding, it passes. Agents must read the full SC text.

antipattern.description: Agent ships an "overlay/widget" as a fix.
antipattern.reason: Never let an agent ship an overlay or JavaScript "accessibility tool" as a fix — they usually make things worse. Flag and refuse.
